name: Word Counter
description: Check word count, character count, sentence count, paragraph count, and estimated reading time for any text.

faq.question: What can I check with the Word Counter?
faq.answer: You can check total words, characters, characters without spaces, sentences, paragraphs, reading time, speaking time, and average words per sentence.

faq.question: Does the Word Counter save my text?
faq.answer: No. The tool runs in your browser and is designed for quick private checks without an account.

faq.question: When should I use a word counter?
faq.answer: Use it when editing blog posts, essays, landing pages, emails, product descriptions, or social copy that needs a specific length.

guide.title: Check content length before you publish.
intro: The Word Counter helps you review the size and structure of a draft before it goes live. Use it for blog posts, landing pages, essays, emails, product descriptions, and social captions.
It shows word count, character count, sentence count, paragraph count, reading time, speaking time, and average sentence length so you can edit with better context.
guide.stepsTitle: How to use the Word Counter

steps.title: Paste your text
steps.text: Add the draft you want to review into the text area.

steps.title: Check the main counts
steps.text: Review total words, characters, sentences, paragraphs, and estimated reading time.

steps.title: Compare with your goal
steps.text: Match the length to the platform, assignment, SEO brief, or campaign requirement.

steps.title: Edit for clarity
steps.text: Trim repeated sections, split long paragraphs, or expand thin areas where the draft needs more detail.

guide.exampleIntro: A short landing page draft can be reviewed quickly before publishing.

example.label: What to check
example.value: Paragraphs, reading time, and sentence length

example.label: Next step
example.value: Shorten repeated copy and keep the call to action clear

bestPractices: Use word count as an editing signal, not a quality score.
Check both words and characters when writing for platforms with limits.
Review average sentence length when copy feels difficult to read.
Use paragraph count to break large blocks into easier sections.
guide.relatedText: Use these guides to improve readability, keyword balance, and content structure.

name: Readability Checker
description: Review how easy your text is to read and improve clarity for blogs, academic writing, emails, and web content.

faq.question: What does the Readability Checker measure?
faq.answer: It reviews words, sentences, syllables, Flesch Reading Ease, Flesch-Kincaid Grade, and a simple reading level label.

faq.question: What readability score should I aim for?
faq.answer: For general web content, a clear and accessible score is usually better than a complex one. Match the reading level to your audience.

faq.question: Can readability affect SEO?
faq.answer: Clear writing can improve user experience, time on page, and conversions, which can support better content performance.

guide.title: Make content easier to understand.
intro: The Readability Checker reviews how easy your writing is to read. It is useful for blog posts, academic drafts, landing pages, support content, emails, and any page where clarity matters.
Use the score as a practical editing signal. A lower grade level is not always better, but confusing sentences, dense paragraphs, and unnecessary jargon usually reduce performance.
guide.stepsTitle: How to use the Readability Checker

steps.title: Paste the draft
steps.text: Add the content you want to review into the checker.

steps.title: Review the scores
steps.text: Look at Flesch Reading Ease, grade level, word count, sentence count, and syllable estimates.

steps.title: Find hard sections
steps.text: If the score is too difficult for your audience, look for long sentences and complex phrasing.

steps.title: Rewrite naturally
steps.text: Split long ideas, use clearer words, and keep paragraphs short enough to scan.

guide.exampleIntro: A marketing email should usually be quick to understand.

example.label: Warning sign
example.value: Very long sentences or dense paragraphs

example.label: Next step
example.value: Shorten sentences and remove unnecessary jargon

bestPractices: Match the reading level to the audience and topic.
Use short paragraphs for web pages and mobile readers.
Read important copy out loud before publishing.
Keep technical terms only when they are useful and explained.
guide.relatedText: Continue with practical guidance on readability scores and clearer writing.

name: Keyword Density Checker
description: Use a free keyword density checker to review keyword frequency, spot repetition, and keep SEO content natural before publishing.

faq.question: What is keyword density?
faq.answer: Keyword density shows how often a word or phrase appears compared with the total word count of your content.

faq.question: Is there a perfect keyword density for SEO?
faq.answer: No single percentage guarantees rankings. Use density as a diagnostic to catch repetition and keep your writing natural.

faq.question: What does this checker help me find?
faq.answer: It helps identify repeated terms, top keywords, unique word count, and possible overuse in SEO-focused content.

guide.title: Check keyword use without writing for robots.
intro: This keyword density checker helps you see how often important words appear in your content. It is useful when you want to catch accidental repetition, compare keyword balance, or review an article before publishing.
Use it as a keyword density checker tool, keyword density analyzer, or SEO keyword density checker when you need a quick look at how naturally your target terms appear in a draft.
guide.stepsTitle: How to use the Keyword Density Checker

steps.title: Paste your draft
steps.text: Add your blog post, landing page copy, product description, or article draft into the text box.

steps.title: Review total and unique words
steps.text: Start with the overall word count and unique word count so you understand the size and variety of the text.

steps.title: Check repeated terms
steps.text: Look at the top words and phrases. If one term appears too often, read those sentences again and decide whether they sound natural.

steps.title: Compare with search intent
steps.text: A keyword density tool can show frequency, but your judgment decides whether the page actually answers the reader’s question.

steps.title: Edit for clarity first
steps.text: Use the results as a signal, not a target. Replace forced repetition with clear wording, examples, and related terms.

guide.exampleIntro: If a 1,000 word article uses the same exact keyword 35 times, the density is 3.5 percent. That number is not automatically bad, but it is a reason to reread the page and check whether the phrasing feels forced.

example.label: Next step
example.value: Read naturally and edit only forced repetition

bestPractices: Do not chase a perfect keyword density percentage.
Use your main keyword in important places, then write naturally through the rest of the page.
Mix exact terms with related language when it helps the reader.
Avoid repeating the same phrase in every paragraph.
Use a keyword density calculator as a review tool, not as a writing rule.
Focus on search intent, clarity, and complete answers before keyword frequency.
guide.relatedText: These guides explain keyword density, safe SEO ranges, checking workflows, keyword stuffing, and natural keyword use.

name: Meta Tag Generator
description: Create clean meta titles, descriptions, and essential head tags for pages that need better search presentation.

faq.question: What meta tags can this tool generate?
faq.answer: It can generate title, description, canonical, robots, Open Graph, and Twitter card tags for a web page.

faq.question: How long should a meta title be?
faq.answer: A practical target is usually around 50 to 60 characters so the title stays clear in search results.

faq.question: Why do meta descriptions matter?
faq.answer: Meta descriptions help set expectations in search results and can improve click-through rate when they are specific and useful.

guide.title: Create search snippets that are easier to click.
intro: The Meta Tag Generator helps you draft page titles, meta descriptions, canonical tags, robots tags, Open Graph tags, and Twitter card tags for web pages.
Use it when publishing a new article, tool, landing page, product page, or campaign page that needs a clear search and social preview.
guide.stepsTitle: How to use the Meta Tag Generator

steps.title: Enter the page title
steps.text: Write a specific title that explains what the page offers.

steps.title: Add the description
steps.text: Summarize the page value in natural language, usually around 120 to 155 characters.

steps.title: Add the canonical URL
steps.text: Use the final public URL of the page so search engines understand the preferred version.

steps.title: Copy the tags
steps.text: Review the generated code and paste it into the page head or template.

guide.exampleIntro: A tool page needs a short title and a clear reason to click.

example.label: Title goal
example.value: Describe the tool in under 60 characters

example.label: Description goal
example.value: Explain the benefit in one sentence

example.label: Next step
example.value: Check that title and description are unique

bestPractices: Write a unique title and description for every important page.
Put the main topic near the start of the title.
Avoid stuffing several keywords into one title.
Make the description useful enough that a searcher wants to click.
guide.relatedText: Learn how titles and descriptions affect search results and click-through rate.

name: CPC & ROAS Calculator
description: Use this free ROAS Calculator to estimate return on ad spend, CPC, CPA, conversion rate, and campaign profit from your ad results.

faq.question: What is CPC?
faq.answer: CPC means cost per click. It shows how much you pay on average for each click from an ad campaign.

faq.question: What is ROAS?
faq.answer: ROAS means return on ad spend. It compares campaign revenue against ad spend to estimate efficiency.

faq.question: What numbers do I need for this calculator?
faq.answer: Enter ad spend, clicks, conversions, and revenue to calculate CPC, CPA, conversion rate, ROAS, and profit.

guide.title: Calculate return on ad spend before increasing your ad budget.
intro: The ROAS Calculator helps you turn basic campaign numbers into a clear return on ad spend result. Add your ad spend, clicks, conversions, and revenue, then review ROAS, CPC, CPA, conversion rate, and simple profit in one place.

Use it as a quick return on ad spend calculator before scaling paid search, paid social, display, or ecommerce campaigns. It is especially helpful when you want to compare campaigns using the same date range and the same revenue source.
guide.stepsTitle: How to use the ROAS Calculator

steps.title: Enter your ad spend
steps.text: Add the total amount spent on the campaign or ad set you want to review.

steps.title: Add clicks and conversions
steps.text: Use the click and conversion numbers from your ad platform or analytics report.

steps.title: Enter campaign revenue
steps.text: Add the revenue connected to the same campaign period so the ROAS formula stays accurate.

steps.title: Review ROAS and profit
steps.text: Use the result to decide whether the campaign is ready to scale, needs testing, or should be paused.

guide.exampleIntro: Here is a simple campaign review using the standard ROAS formula.
bestPractices: Compare campaigns using the same date range and attribution window.
Review profit alongside ROAS when product cost, shipping, or service delivery costs matter.
Do not scale a campaign only because ROAS looks high; check conversion volume and data quality too.
Use UTM tracking links so revenue can be matched to the right campaign source.
guide.relatedText: Use these guides to understand the ROAS formula, common benchmarks, and the difference between ROAS and ROI.

name: UTM Builder
description: Use this free UTM Builder to create tracking URLs for Google Analytics, ads, email, social posts, and campaign reporting.

faq.question: What is a UTM Builder?
faq.answer: A UTM Builder creates a tracking URL by adding UTM parameters such as source, medium, and campaign to your normal website link.

faq.question: Can I use this UTM Builder for Google Analytics?
faq.answer: Yes. The generated campaign URLs can be used with Google Analytics and GA4 reports to identify campaign traffic.

faq.question: Which UTM fields are required?
faq.answer: Source, medium, and campaign are the most important fields. Term and content are optional for keywords, ads, buttons, or creative versions.

faq.question: When should I create a tracking URL?
faq.answer: Create a tracking URL before sharing links in email, paid ads, social posts, QR codes, newsletters, or partner campaigns.

faq.question: Should I use UTM parameters on internal links?
faq.answer: No. UTM parameters are best for external campaign links. Using them on internal website navigation can make analytics reports messy.

guide.title: Create clean campaign tracking links without manual URL mistakes.
intro: This UTM Builder helps you create tracking URLs for Google Analytics, GA4 reports, paid ads, email campaigns, social posts, partner links, and QR campaigns. Instead of typing UTM parameters by hand, you can enter your campaign details and generate a clean URL that is easier to measure later.
Use it as a UTM Generator, UTM Link Builder, Campaign URL Builder, Google Analytics URL Builder, or Tracking Link Generator whenever you need consistent UTM tracking across marketing channels.
guide.stepsTitle: How to use the UTM Builder

steps.title: Paste the destination URL
steps.text: Start with the final page you want visitors to reach, such as a landing page, product page, article, signup form, or offer page.

steps.title: Add campaign source
steps.text: Use the platform or sender as the source. Common examples include google, facebook, linkedin, newsletter, partner, or instagram.

steps.title: Add campaign medium
steps.text: Use the channel type as the medium. Common examples include cpc, email, social, referral, display, or qr.

steps.title: Name the campaign clearly
steps.text: Choose a campaign name that will still make sense later, such as summer_sale, ebook_launch, black_friday, or product_demo.

steps.title: Copy and test the tracking URL
steps.text: Copy the generated URL, open it in a new tab, and confirm it loads correctly before using it in ads, emails, posts, or QR codes.

guide.exampleIntro: For a newsletter campaign promoting a new guide, your tracking fields could look like this:
bestPractices: Use lowercase names so reports do not split the same source into multiple versions.
Use underscores or hyphens instead of spaces in UTM parameters.
Keep source, medium, and campaign naming consistent across every channel.
Use clear source names such as google, newsletter, linkedin, facebook, partner, or qr.
Do not add UTM parameters to internal links on your own website.
Document your naming rules if more than one person creates campaign URLs.
Use utm_content only when you need to compare buttons, creatives, placements, or link versions.
guide.relatedText: These guides explain the concepts behind campaign URLs, UTM parameters, Google Analytics tracking, and tracking URL examples.

name: QR Code Generator
description: Generate QR codes for links, landing pages, forms, promotions, product pages, and campaign access points.

faq.question: What can I put inside a QR code?
faq.answer: You can encode URLs, campaign links, messages, email addresses, phone numbers, forms, and other short text content.

faq.question: Which QR size should I choose?
faq.answer: Use a larger size for print or posters, and keep enough margin around the code so phones can scan it reliably.

faq.question: Can I download the QR code?
faq.answer: Yes. The tool supports downloading generated QR codes as PNG or SVG files.

guide.title: Create scannable QR codes for campaigns and links.
intro: The QR Code Generator creates QR codes for URLs, forms, landing pages, offers, menus, events, and campaign links. It is useful when people need to move from print, packaging, signage, or offline materials to a web page.
For marketing work, combine QR codes with UTM tracking so scans can be measured inside analytics reports.
guide.stepsTitle: How to use the QR Code Generator

steps.title: Enter the content
steps.text: Paste the URL, campaign link, or short text you want to encode.

steps.title: Choose the size
steps.text: Use larger sizes for print, posters, flyers, and packaging.

steps.title: Generate and test
steps.text: Create the QR code and scan it with a phone before publishing.

steps.title: Download the file
steps.text: Save the QR code as PNG or SVG depending on where it will be used.

guide.exampleIntro: A printed flyer can send visitors to a tracked landing page.

example.label: Content
example.value: Landing page URL with UTM tags

example.label: Format
example.value: SVG for print or PNG for quick sharing

bestPractices: Always test the QR code before printing or sharing.
Keep enough white space around the code so phones can scan it.
Use a short, stable URL when possible.
Add UTM parameters when the QR code is part of a campaign.
guide.relatedText: Use UTM Builder guides when QR scans need campaign tracking.

name: QR / Barcode Scanner
description: Scan QR codes and supported barcodes through mobile or desktop camera workflows where HTTPS is available.

faq.question: Can this scanner use my camera?
faq.answer: Yes, supported browsers can scan QR codes through the camera when the site is opened over HTTPS.

faq.question: Can I scan an uploaded image?
faq.answer: Yes. You can upload an image containing a QR code or supported barcode and the tool will try to decode it.

faq.question: Why might scanning fail?
faq.answer: Scanning can fail when the image is blurry, too small, poorly lit, cropped, or uses a barcode format the browser does not support.
